text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic cystectomy by opening the cyst located in the posterior part of the left broad ligament. Dark, chocolate-colored blood is visible as it is removed from the cyst, with care taken to avoid using energy devices near the ovary to preserve follicle count.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to remove pathological attachments between the left ovary and the posterior part of the broad ligament. The surgical field reveals the left fallopian tube, infundibulopelvic ligament, round ligament, and left ovary. The utero-ovarian ligament is visible, with abnormal attachments present. After freeing the ovary, the surgeon proceeds with cystectomy, aiming to remove the cyst while preserving ovarian follicles by carefully separating the ovarian cortex from the endometriotic cyst.
The surgeon carefully dissects between the ovarian cortex and the endometriosis cyst, aiming for a bloodless separation by staying close to the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon uses millimetric traction with forceps to carefully dissect the cyst from the ovarian cortex, aiming for a bloodless separation. The video shows the cyst and ovarian cortex with visible adhesions in zone two, where the risk of damaging the ovarian cortex is higher due to increased adhesion. Some bleeding is observed in this deeper plane.

The surgeon carefully navigates dense adhesions in zone three of the ovarian endometriosis cyst, identifying the cyst and ovarian cortex. The surgical plane is visible, requiring meticulous dissection to minimize bleeding and preserve ovarian function.

The surgeon performs a bloodless dissection of the endometriosis cyst, gently retracting the ovarian cortex with forceps to maintain traction. The surgical field shows the cyst and ovarian cortex, with adjustments in forceps placement to optimize traction and minimize trauma. When bleeding occurs, the surgeon applies forceps directly to the bleeder without using energy devices, continuing the careful dissection.

The surgeon identifies a bleeder on the ovary and opts for hemostasis using a monocryl 2-0 stitch instead of bipolar energy. The stitch is placed in the cortical part of the ovary, beginning from the middle part, to reconstruct normal anatomy and reduce the risk of abnormal adhesion.

The surgeon stitches the cut ovarian cortex, aiming to invaginate the previous bed of the cyst without creating a tight knot, to avoid ischemia. The final knot is secured using the Entracofoil knotting technique, completing the hemostasis of the ovary.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field for additional endometriosis lesions, confirming the presence of a solitary cyst. The small cyst is extracted directly through the trocar without an endo bag. The area is irrigated post-extraction, and the anatomy is reviewed, focusing on the posterior part of the left broad ligament.
The surgeon controls hemostasis using bipolar coagulation on a bleeding lesion, taking care to avoid damaging the uterus during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the ureter, ensuring careful handling to avoid damage during the procedure. A small lesion is visible near the ureter, highlighting the importance of imaging for detecting additional endometriotic lesions.
The surgeon removes all fluid from the pelvic cavity, marking the final step of the procedure.
